title: Psalms 31
subtitle: Into Your Hands I Commit My Spirit

heb.paragraph: "Into your hands I commit my spirit (rūḥî)" (v.5). Jesus’ last words from the cross (Luke 23:46). David entrusts his entire being — breath, life-force, inner self — to God’s keeping. The ultimate act of trust: giving your spirit to the one who gave it.

"How abundant are the good things that you have stored up for those who fear you" (v.19). The psalm alternates between desperate lament and extravagant confidence. David is besieged, forgotten like the dead, surrounded by terror (vv.9–13). Yet: "I trust in you, LORD; I say, ‘You are my God.’ My times are in your hands" (vv.14–15).

panels.ctx: David takes refuge in God (v.1), asks for deliverance (v.2), commits his spirit (v.5), and then describes his distress: eyes weak with sorrow, life consumed by anguish, strength failing, bones wasting (vv.9–10). He is forgotten like the dead, broken like pottery (v.12). Terror on every side (v.13). Yet the pivot: "But I trust in you, LORD. My times are in your hands" (vv.14–15).

cross.note: "Father, into your hands I commit my spirit" — Jesus’ final words quoting Psalm 31:5.
"I hear many whispering, ‘Terror on every side!’" — Jeremiah echoes David’s language.

notes.note: "Into your hands I commit my spirit." Jesus made this his dying prayer. The most sacred sentence in the Psalter — the last words of the Son of God.
"I am forgotten as though I were dead; I have become like broken pottery." The imagery: a dead person erased from memory, a smashed pot beyond repair.
"But I trust in you, LORD. My times are in your hands." The great "but" of faith. Times — all of them, every season, including death — belong to God.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the brevity is its power. Five Hebrew words. No explanation, no negotiation. Pure transfer of trust.
Calvin: this is the prayer every believer should die with. Calvin: if the Son committed his spirit to the Father, so should every child of God.
Calvin: "my times" includes the time of death. Calvin: whoever can say "my times are in your hands" has conquered the fear of death.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: rūaḥ (spirit) is the breath/life-force God gave at creation (Gen 2:7). Committing it back is returning what was always God’s.
The NET: "terror on every side" (māgōr missābîb) becomes Jeremiah’s signature phrase (Jer 6:25; 20:3, 10; 46:5). David coined it; Jeremiah lived it.
Kidner: Jesus’ use of this verse transformed it from a psalm of refuge into the prayer of the dying. Every Christian who has died reciting it follows Christ.
VanGemeren: "into your hands" places the entire person — spirit, body, future — under divine custody. The hands that formed creation hold the spirit that returns to them.
Goldingay: "my times are in your hands" is the psalm’s theological centre. Not my plans, not my schedule, not my preferred timeline — but my actual times, as God assigns them.

heb.paragraph: "How abundant is your goodness (ṭūb) that you have stored up for those who fear you" (v.19). Stored up — like treasure reserved, accumulated, waiting. God’s goodness is not improvised but prepared in advance. The suffering of vv.9–18 is answered by the hidden treasure of v.19.

panels.ctx: The psalm’s second half erupts in praise. God’s goodness is stored up (v.19), his people are sheltered in his presence from intrigues and accusation (v.20). "Praise be to the LORD, for he showed me the wonders of his love when I was in a city under siege" (v.21). The closing exhortation: "Be strong and take heart, all you who hope in the LORD" (v.24).

cross.note: "In all things God works for the good of those who love him" — the stored-up goodness theology.
"Every spiritual blessing in Christ" — blessings stored up before the foundation of the world.

notes.note: "How abundant is your goodness, stored up for those who fear you." Goodness exists before you experience it. It is already prepared.
"He showed me the wonders of his love when I was in a city under siege." The most desperate circumstances are when God’s love becomes most wonderful.
"Be strong and take heart, all you who hope in the LORD." The psalm ends with a call to courage for all who wait.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "stored up" (ṣāfantā) is the verb for hiding treasure. God’s goodness is cached, secured, reserved for the right moment.
Calvin: the stored goodness means that suffering does not indicate God’s poverty. He has resources the sufferer cannot yet see. Faith trusts the treasury before seeing the withdrawal.
Calvin: "be strong and take heart" is addressed to the congregation. Calvin: one person’s testimony of deliverance strengthens the entire community.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: ṣāfan means to hide, to treasure up, to store away. The same root appears in Proverbs 2:7: "he holds success in store for the upright."
The NET: the imperative "be strong" (ḥizqū) is the same verb used for Joshua: "be strong and courageous" (Josh 1:6). The call to courage echoes the conquest.
Kidner: "stored up" is the language of hidden treasure. The suffering person does not see the goodness yet — but it is already there, laid aside, waiting.
VanGemeren: the stored-goodness theology transforms suffering: what looks like absence is actually accumulation. God is preparing what he will reveal.
Goldingay: the exhortation shifts from David’s personal testimony to communal encouragement. His experience becomes everyone’s resource.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: David is a key figure in Into Your Hands I Commit My Spirit.

ppl.name: God
ppl.text: The most sacred sentence in the Psalter — the last words of the Son of God…

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI will be glad and rejoice in your love, for you saw my affliction and knew the anguish of my soul.ESVI will rejoice and be glad in your steadfast love, because you have seen my affliction; you have known the distress of my soul,NIVSince you are my rock and my fortress, for the sake of your name lead and guide me.ESVFor you are my rock and my fortress; and for your name\u2019s sake you lead me and guide me;

rec.text: ""Into your hands I commit my spirit." Jesus made this his dying prayer. The most sacred sentence in the Psalter — the last words of the Son of God."

rows.label: Stored Goodness — 31:19–24
rows.text: "How abundant is your goodness, stored up." Love in a siege. "Be strong, all who hope."

hebtext.note: "Into your hands I commit my spirit (rūḥî)" (v.5). Jesus’ last words from the cross (Luke 23:46). David entrusts his entire being — breath, life-force, inner self — to God’s keeping. The ultimate act of trust: giving your spirit to the one who gave it.

David is besieged, forgotten like the dead, surrounded by terror (vv.9–13). Yet: "I trust in you, LORD; I say, ‘You are my God.’ My times are in your hands" (vv.14–15).

themes.note: Faith peaks: "into your hands" is the ultimate trust. Prophecy: Jesus’ last words from the cross. Mercy: stored-up goodness revealed in siege.
